q: More than 3 billion people depend on ___ and coastal biodiversity for their livelihoods.
The loss of biodiversity affects the lives of more than 1 billion people living in ___.
Biodiversity is under threat in many aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems as the human ___ continues to increase.
The destruction of the natural environment leads to ___.
The clearing of land for agriculture, housing, transport, leisure facilities and industry removes ___.
When habitats become divided into small areas, it is known as ___.
Physically ___ species are more sensitive to reduction in habitat area.
Major reasons for habitat destruction include large scale industrial and commercial activities, mining, cattle rearing, commercial fishing and ___.
Plantation ___ and agriculture are major reasons for habitat destruction.
Myanmar is a large agricultural country, however, most cultivation employs techniques that can significantly ___ the natural environment.
___ in upland areas and gold mining are examples of land-use change in Myanmar.
Habitat loss is the most significant cause of ___ loss globally.
Factors such as the replacement of local landraces with modern varieties threaten the ___ of Myanmar.
Overgrazing, dams and canal construction and ___ are major threats to the agrobiodiversity of Myanmar.
Pollution is caused by uncontrolled ___ and herbicide uses.
IAS stands for ___.
IAS can also be introduced unintentionally by ___ or through the transport of cargo.
Water-hyacinth is an invasive alien ___ species in Myanmar.
___ indicates the loss of identity of wild plant species as a result of transfer of genes from crop plants.
Gene flow is expected to have its greatest effect on ___ species that are closely related to crop plants.
The introduction of ___ organisms into complex ecosystems is a dangerous global experiment.
Once released, it is virtually impossible to ___ genetically engineered organisms back to the laboratory or the field.
Overexploitation, also called ___, can lead to the destruction of the resource.
___ is the removal of a species of fish from a body of water at a rate greater than the species can replenish its population naturally.
___ is a form of overexploitation caused by legal or illegal logging activities.
Overhunting threatens ___-___ of endangered vertebrates.
___ provides increased opportunity for transmission of several zoonotic viruses from animal hosts to humans.
Rising ocean temperatures and diminishing ___ affects marine biodiversity.
Human ___ leads to additional strains on resources and spikes in pollution.
___ refers to the commerce of products that are derived from non-domesticated animals or plants.
___ wildlife trade contributes directly to the loss of species and habitat destruction.
The most trafficked animal in the world is the ___.
Pangolins are the only ___ mammal on earth.
The primary drivers of the intercontinental pangolin trade are pangolin ___ and scales.
Wildlife smuggling directly affects the biodiversity of different ___.
Outbreaks of ___ including COVID-19, SARS, and monkeypox have been linked to live wildlife markets.
Places where targeted action for wildlife trade protection is particularly worthwhile are called ___.
___ is the international law for wildlife protection.
Myanmar enacted the ___ Law in 2018.
